participants.name: Wei (ailgkm)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-ailgkmur
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-ailgkmur
participants.professional_persona: Wei Ming is a recent computer science graduate who joined a startup building AI tools for small retailers. He is driven by the idea of democratising AI for neighborhood shops.
participants.skills_and_expertise: He has strong machine learning fundamentals, is proficient in PyTorch and cloud deployment, and contributed to an open‑source retail analytics project during university.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wei Ming wants to develop a low‑cost inventory forecasting system for SMEs. He plans to apply for the 100 Experiments co‑funding to make his solution commercially viable.

participants.name: I (4kro9i)
participants.professional_persona: I am a natural language processing specialist at a govtech agency, building chatbots for citizen services. My background in linguistics complements my technical work on low-resource language models.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in transformers (BERT, GPT), tokenisation, and model fine-tuning for multilingual tasks. I also evaluate model bias in public-facing applications.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I aspire to create inclusive AI that serves Singapore's multicultural community. Eventually, I want to lead research in Southeast Asian language AI.

participants.name: Dr. (lji7a2)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Nair is a postdoctoral researcher at SMU, focusing on responsible AI fairness metrics. She actively works with the 100 Experiments programme to audit AI systems for bias in hiring tools.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in algorithmic fairness, bias detection, and socio‑technical evaluation of AI systems.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to become a thought leader in AI ethics and help shape Singapore’s AI governance framework.

participants.name: A (oleawl)
participants.professional_persona: A reinforcement learning researcher turned applied AI engineer in the energy sector, I optimise grid operations to reduce carbon footprint. I left academia after my postdoc to work on tangible climate solutions.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of RL algorithms, simulation environments, and Bayesian optimisation. I code in Julia and Python, and use Kubernetes for large-scale training.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to make Singapore's energy grid AI-optimised and resilient. My dream is to found a clean-tech startup that exports AI solutions for smart grids.

participants.name: Mr. (5v2u6r)
participants.professional_persona: Mr. Teo is an AI research engineer at a deep‑tech startup, working on novel generative models for design automation. He is a former AIAP trainee and now leads a small team in the 100E programme.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Hands‑on with large language models, diffusion models, and MLOps pipelines; strong in rapid iteration and deployment.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Hopes to co‑found a startup that commercialises generative AI tools for SMEs and to foster a community of practice for AI engineers.

initial_posts.narrative: As an NLP engineer in a govtech agency, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as foundational for enabling AI experimentation and adoption. The policy commits over S$500 million for AI R&D, which aligns with the resource logic stating 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D'. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, I estimate 2 million GPU hours available in 2023 based on the need to support both industry and research experiments, given Singapore's investment in compute infrastructure. For trainers, I estimate 50 personnel, considering the need to guide enterprises and practitioners through programmes like 100E and AIAP, though this is not explicitly stated in the policy context.

This is based on a typical pace of 2-3 sessions per week across industry sectors. Training workshops should number around 60, covering both technical AI skills and domain-specific applications. Platform onboarding assists are estimated at 200, reflecting the need to help enterprises and researchers get started on the AI experimentation platform, given the compute infrastructure and co-funding cap per project.

initial_posts.narrative: As an NLP engineer at a govtech agency, I see the 100E programme as a key driver for AI adoption. With S$500 million total funding and 50 trainers, I estimate 100 enterprise outreach sessions in 2023, which is a realistic pace given the need to engage diverse sectors and the capacity of the 50 trainers. This is slightly lower than the 120 estimated by the AI researcher, because I think outreach is resource-intensive and the trainers also need to deliver workshops and onboarding. For matchmaking, I estimate 30 events, as pairing researchers with enterprise challenges requires careful curation and follow-up, and 40 may be too many given the trainer count. Training workshops at 50 align with the 50 trainers, assuming each trainer leads one workshop per year, plus some collaborative sessions. Platform onboarding assists at 150 reflect the need to help enterprises and researchers get started on the AI experimentation platform, leveraging the 2 million GPU hours and co-funding cap, but I keep it moderate because onboarding is time-consuming and the platform may have limited capacity initially.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed how the 100E programme's activities—outreach, matchmaking, workshops, and onboarding—scale from inputs like S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, with causal links to project approvals and deployments. There was consensus that outreach sessions and matchmaking events are resource-intensive, requiring careful curation, and that trainer capacity limits all activities. Disagreement centered on the pace of outreach (100-120 sessions) and matchmaking (30-40 events), with some favoring higher numbers based on ambition and others lower due to practical constraints. Training workshops were consistently estimated at 50-60, and platform onboarding assists at 150-200, with central tendencies around 100 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 50 workshops, and 150 onboarding assists.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher working with the 100E programme, I estimate that 100 projects will be approved in 2023, matching the programme's name and the policy's ambition to generate 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project and total funding of S$500 million support this scale, though not all approved projects will complete in the same year. For AI prototypes, I estimate 80 will be successfully built, as some projects may face technical challenges or delays in development. The 55 training workshops and 180 platform onboarding assists from the activities phase should enable a steady flow of prototypes, but not all will reach a prototype stage within 2023. For trainees completing the programme, I estimate 200, based on the 50 trainers and the policy goal of boosting the AI practitioner pool to 15,000. The 9-month AIAP programme means only one cohort may finish in 2023, and with limited trainer capacity, 200 completions is a realistic number.
